input: What science fantasy young adult series, told in first person, has a set of companion books narrating the stories of enslaved worlds and alien species?

task.prompt: What science fantasy young adult series, told in first person, has a set of companion books narrating the stories of enslaved worlds and alien species?
task.context:
[1] Andre Norton Award
The Andre Norton Award for Young Adult Science Fiction and Fantasy is an annual award presented by the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America (SFWA) to the author of the best young adult or middle grade science fiction or fantasy book published in the United States in the preceding year. It is named to honor prolific science fiction and fantasy author Andre Norton (1912–2005), and it was established by then SFWA president Catherine Asaro and the SFWA Young Adult Fiction committee and announced on February 20, 2005. Any published young adult or middle grade science fiction or fantasy novel is eligible for the prize, including graphic novels. There is no limit on word count. The award is presented along with the Nebula Awards and follows the same rules for nominations and voting; as the awards are separate, works may be simultaneously nominated for both the Andre Norton award and a Nebula Award.

[2] Victoria Hanley
Victoria Hanley is an American young adult fantasy novelist. Her first three books, "The Seer And The Sword", "The Healer's Keep" and "The Light Of The Oracle" are companion books to one another. Her newest book (released March 2012) is the sequel of a series, called "Indigo Magic", published by Egmont USA. She's also published two non-fiction books through Cotton Wood Press; called "Seize the Story: A Handbook For Teens Who Like To Write", and "Wild Ink: A Grownups Guide To Writing Fiction For Teens".

[3] The Hork-Bajir Chronicles
The Hork-Bajir Chronicles is the second companion book to the "Animorphs" series, written by K. A. Applegate. With respect to continuity within the series, it takes place before book #23, "The Pretender", although the events told in the story occur between the time of "The Ellimist Chronicles" and "The Andalite Chronicles". The book is introduced by Tobias, who flies to the valley of the free Hork-Bajir, where Jara Hamee tells him the story of how the Yeerks enslaved the Hork-Bajir, and how Aldrea, an Andalite, and her companion, Dak Hamee, a Hork-Bajir, tried to save their world from the invasion. Jara Hamee's story is narrated from the points of view of Aldrea, Dak Hamee, and Esplin 9466, alternating in similar fashion to the "Megamorphs" books.

[4] Shadowshaper
Shadowshaper is a 2015 American urban fantasy young adult novel written by Daniel José Older. It follows Sierra Santiago, an Afro-Boricua teenager living in Brooklyn. She is the granddaughter of a "shadowshaper", or a person who infuses art with ancestral spirits. As forces of gentrification invade their community and a mysterious being who appropriates their magic begins to hunt the aging shadowshapers, Sierra must learn about her artistic and spiritual heritage to foil the killer.

[5] Left Behind: The Kids
"Left Behind: The Kids (stylized as LEFT BEHIND >THE KIDS<)" is a series written by Jerry B. Jenkins, Tim LaHaye, and Chris Fabry. The series consists of 40 short novels aimed primarily at the young adult market based on the adult series Left Behind also written by Jerry B. Jenkins. It follows a core group of teenagers as they experience the rapture and tribulation, based on scriptures found in the Bible, and background plots introduced in the adult novels. Like the adult series, the books were published by Tyndale House Publishing, and released over the 7 year period of 1997-2004. The series has sold over 11 million copies worldwide.

[6] List of Square Enix companion books
Dozens of Square Enix companion books have been produced since 1998, when video game developer Square began to produce books that focused on artwork, developer interviews, and background information on the fictional worlds and characters in its games rather than on gameplay details. The first series of these books was the "Perfect Works" series, written and published by Square subsidiary DigiCube. They produced three books between 1998 and 1999 before the line was stopped in favor of the "Ultimania" (アルティマニア , Arutimania ) series, a portmanteau of ultimate and mania. This series of books is written by Studio BentStuff, which had previously written game guides for Square for "Final Fantasy VII". They were published by DigiCube until the company was dissolved in 2003. Square merged with video game publisher Enix on April 1, 2003 to form Square Enix, which resumed publication of the companion books.

[7] The Divide trilogy
The Divide trilogy is a fantasy young adult novel trilogy by Elizabeth Kay, which takes place in an alternate universe. The three books are "The Divide" (2002), "Back to The Divide" (2005), and "Jinx on The Divide" (2006). The first novel was originally published by the small press publisher Chicken House (now a division of Scholastic), with subsequent volumes published by Scholastic, which also reprinted the first novel. The books have been translated into French, German, Spanish, Finnish, Chinese, Japanese, Portuguese, Italian, Romanian and Dutch. Interior illustrations are by Ted Dewan.

[8] Science Fantasy (magazine)
Science Fantasy, which also appeared under the titles Impulse and SF Impulse, was a British fantasy and science fiction magazine, launched in 1950 by Nova Publications as a companion to Nova's "New Worlds". Walter Gillings was editor for the first two issues, and was then replaced by John Carnell, the editor of "New Worlds", as a cost-saving measure. Carnell edited both magazines until Nova went out of business in early 1964. The titles were acquired by Roberts & Vinter, who hired Kyril Bonfiglioli to edit "Science Fantasy"; Bonfiglioli changed the title to "Impulse" in early 1966, but the new title led to confusion with the distributors and sales fell, though the magazine remained profitable. The title was changed again to "SF Impulse" for the last few issues. "Science Fantasy" ceased publication the following year, when Roberts & Vinter came under financial pressure after their printer went bankrupt.

[9] Animorphs
Animorphs is a science fantasy series of young adult books written by Katherine Applegate and her husband Michael Grant, writing together under the name K. A. Applegate, and published by Scholastic. It is told in first person, with all six main characters taking turns narrating the books through their own perspectives. Horror, war, dehumanization, sanity, morality, innocence, leadership, freedom and growing up are the core themes of the series.

[10] Etiquette & Espionage
Etiquette & Espionage is a young adult steampunk novel by Gail Carriger. It is her first young adult novel, and is set in the same universe as her bestselling Parasol Protectorate adult series.
task.deliverable: Answer the question using only the provided context. Start with a short answer phrase, then briefly justify it.
evaluation_criteria: Answer should match the gold answer under official normalization.
Reasoning should rely on the provided context.
references: List of Square Enix companion books

steps.response:
Problem Restatement:  
Identify a science fantasy young adult book series with a first-person narrative that includes companion books focusing on enslaved alien species and their worlds.  

Success Criteria:  
- Confirm the series is explicitly labeled as "science fantasy."  
- Verify the series uses a first-person narrative structure.  
- Ensure companion books detail stories of enslaved alien species or worlds.  

Plan:  
1. Scan the context for series named "Animorphs" (entry 9), which is described as a science fantasy YA series in first-person.  
2. Check if companion books (e.g., *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* in entry 3) align with the enslaved worlds/aliens criterion.  
3. Validate that no other series (e.g., *Left Behind: The Kids*, *Shadowshaper*) matches all criteria.  

Risks and Unknowns:  
- Ambiguity about whether "companion books" in the question refer to standalone works or parts of a series.  
- Potential overlap between "science fantasy" and other genres (e.g., steampunk in *Etiquette & Espionage*).  
- Uncertainty if *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is a companion to *Animorphs* or a separate series.  

Research Brief:  
Focus on *Animorphs* (entry 9) and its companion *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* (entry 3). Confirm: (1) *Animorphs* is science fantasy, first-person, and YA; (2) *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* explicitly details enslaved Hork-Bajir and their world. Exclude other entries lacking all criteria.

- Do not write the final polished answer.
- Do not ignore uncertainty.

Research Handoff:

Identify a science fantasy young adult book series with a first-person narrative that includes companion books focusing on enslaved alien species and their worlds.  

Success Criteria:  
- Confirm the series is explicitly labeled as "science fantasy."  
- Verify the series uses a first-person narrative structure.  
- Ensure companion books detail stories of enslaved alien species or worlds.

1. Scan the context for series named "Animorphs" (entry 9), which is described as a science fantasy YA series in first-person.  
2. Check if companion books (e.g., *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* in entry 3) align with the enslaved worlds/aliens criterion.  
3. Validate that no other series (e.g., *Left Behind: The Kids*, *Shadowshaper*) matches all criteria.  

Risks and Unknowns:  
- Ambiguity about whether "companion books" in the question refer to standalone works or parts of a series.  
- Potential overlap between "science fantasy" and other genres (e.g., steampunk in *Etiquette & Espionage*).  
- Uncertainty if *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is a companion to *Animorphs* or a separate series.  

Research Brief:  
Focus on *Animorphs* (entry 9) and its companion *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* (entry 3). Confirm: (1) *Animorphs* is science fantasy, first-person, and YA; (2) *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* explicitly details enslaved Hork-Bajir and their world. Exclude other entries lacking all criteria.
</coordinator_plan>

steps.response:
Evidence and Observations:  
- **Animorphs (entry 9)** is explicitly labeled as a "science fantasy" series, written for young adults, and told in first person with multiple narrators.  
- **The Hork-Bajir Chronicles (entry 3)** is described as a companion book to *Animorphs*, focusing on the enslaved Hork-Bajir species and their world, narrated from the perspectives of enslaved characters.  
- Other entries (e.g., *Left Behind: The Kids*, *Shadowshaper*) do not align with all criteria (e.g., lack science fantasy label, first-person narrative, or focus on enslaved alien worlds).  

Options:  
- The primary candidate is *Animorphs* with its companion *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles*, as they meet the science fantasy, first-person, and enslaved alien world criteria.  
- Alternative possibilities include Victoria Hanley’s companion books (entry 2) or Square Enix companion works (entry 6), but these lack explicit ties to enslaved alien species or science fantasy framing.  

Assumptions:  
- **Companion books** in the question refer to works directly tied to the main series (e.g., *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* as a companion to *Animorphs*).  
- The term "science fantasy" in the question aligns with the genre label applied to *Animorphs* (entry 9), even though other entries (e.g., *Etiquette & Espionage*) blend genres.  
- The enslaved Hork-Bajir in *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* are explicitly tied to the broader *Animorphs* narrative, though the context does not clarify if this is a standalone companion or part of a larger series.  

Open Questions:  
- Is *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* a standalone companion book or part of a larger series tied to *Animorphs*?  
- Could other entries (e.g., Square Enix companion books) be interpreted as science fantasy with enslaved alien themes, even if not explicitly labeled as such?  
- Are there unresolved ambiguities about whether "companion books" in the question refer to supplementary works or standalone series?  

Research Handoff:  
Prioritize verifying the relationship between *Animorphs* and *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* (e.g., whether the latter is a direct companion series) and confirming that no other entry meets all criteria (science fantasy, first-person, enslaved alien worlds). Focus on entries 3 and 9, with secondary review of entries 2 and 6 for alternative possibilities.

The book is introduced by Tobias, who flies to the valley of the free Hork-Bajir, where Jara Hamee tells him the story of how the Yeerks enslaved the Hork-Bajir, and how Aldrea, an Andalite, and her companion, Dak Hamee, a Hork-Bajir, tried to save their world from the invasion. Jara Hamee's story is narrated from the points of view of Aldrea, Dak Hamee, and Esplin 9466, alternating in similar fashion to the "Megamorphs" books.

[4] Shadowshaper
Shadowshaper is a 2015 American urban fantasy young adult novel written by Daniel José Older. It follows Sierra Santiago, an Afro-Boricua teenager living in Brooklyn. She is the granddaughter of a "shadowshaper", or a person who infuses art with ancestral spirits. As forces of gentrification invade their community and a mysterious being who appropriates their magic begins to hunt the aging shadowshapers, Sierra must learn about her artistic and spiritual heritage to foil the killer.

[5] Left Behind: The Kids
"Left Behind: The Kids (stylized as LEFT BEHIND >THE KIDS<)" is a series written by Jerry B. Jenkins, Tim LaHaye, and Chris Fabry. The series consists of 40 short novels aimed primarily at the young adult market based on the adult series Left Behind also written by Jerry B. Jenkins. It follows a core group of teenagers as they experience the rapture and tribulation, based on scriptures found in the Bible, and background plots introduced in the adult novels. Like the adult series, the books were published by Tyndale House Publishing, and released over the 7 year period of 1997-2004. The series has sold over 11 million copies worldwide.

[6] List of Square Enix companion books
Dozens of Square Enix companion books have been produced since 1998, when video game developer Square began to produce books that focused on artwork, developer interviews, and background information on the fictional worlds and characters in its games rather than on gameplay details. The first series of these books was the "Perfect Works" series, written and published by Square subsidiary DigiCube. They produced three books between 1998 and 1999 before the line was stopped in favor of the "Ultimania" (アルティマニア , Arutimania ) series, a portmanteau of ultimate and mania. This series of books is written by Studio BentStuff, which had previously written game guides for Square for "Final Fantasy VII". They were published by DigiCube until the company was dissolved in 2003. Square merged with video game publisher Enix on April 1, 2003 to form Square Enix, which resumed publication of the companion books.

[7] The Divide trilogy
The Divide trilogy is a fantasy young adult novel trilogy by Elizabeth Kay, which takes place in an alternate universe. The three books are "The Divide" (2002), "Back to The Divide" (2005), and "Jinx on The Divide" (2006). The first novel was originally published by the small press publisher Chicken House (now a division of Scholastic), with subsequent volumes published by Scholastic, which also reprinted the first novel. The books have been translated into French, German, Spanish, Finnish, Chinese, Japanese, Portuguese, Italian, Romanian and Dutch. Interior illustrations are by Ted Dewan.

[8] Science Fantasy (magazine)
Science Fantasy, which also appeared under the titles Impulse and SF Impulse, was a British fantasy and science fiction magazine, launched in 1950 by Nova Publications as a companion to Nova's "New Worlds". Walter Gillings was editor for the first two issues, and was then replaced by John Carnell, the editor of "New Worlds", as a cost-saving measure. Carnell edited both magazines until Nova went out of business in early 1964. The titles were acquired by Roberts & Vinter, who hired Kyril Bonfiglioli to edit "Science Fantasy"; Bonfiglioli changed the title to "Impulse" in early 1966, but the new title led to confusion with the distributors and sales fell, though the magazine remained profitable. The title was changed again to "SF Impulse" for the last few issues. "Science Fantasy" ceased publication the following year, when Roberts & Vinter came under financial pressure after their printer went bankrupt.

[9] Animorphs
Animorphs is a science fantasy series of young adult books written by Katherine Applegate and her husband Michael Grant, writing together under the name K. A. Applegate, and published by Scholastic. It is told in first person, with all six main characters taking turns narrating the books through their own perspectives. Horror, war, dehumanization, sanity, morality, innocence, leadership, freedom and growing up are the core themes of the series.

[10] Etiquette & Espionage
Etiquette & Espionage is a young adult steampunk novel by Gail Carriger. It is her first young adult novel, and is set in the same universe as her bestselling Parasol Protectorate adult series.
</task_context>

<deliverable>
Answer the question using only the provided context. Start with a short answer phrase, then briefly justify it.
</deliverable>

</evaluation_criteria>

Problem Restatement:  
Identify a science fantasy young adult book series with a first-person narrative that includes companion books focusing on enslaved alien species and their worlds.

- Confirm the series is explicitly labeled as "science fantasy."

3. Validate that no other series (e.g., *Left Behind: The Kids*, *Shadowshaper*) matches all criteria.  

Risks and Unknowns:  
- Ambiguity about whether "companion books" in the question refer to standalone works or parts of a series.  
- Potential overlap between "science fantasy" and other genres (e.g., steampunk in *Etiquette & Espionage*).  
- Uncertainty if *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is a companion to *Animorphs* or a separate series.  

Research Brief:  
Focus on *Animorphs* (entry 9) and its companion *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* (entry 3). Confirm: (1) *Animorphs* is science fantasy, first-person, and YA; (2) *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* explicitly details enslaved Hork-Bajir and their world. Exclude other entries lacking all criteria.
</coordinator_plan>

<researcher_notes>
Evidence and Observations:  
- **Animorphs (entry 9)** is explicitly labeled as a "science fantasy" series, written for young adults, and told in first person with multiple narrators.  
- **The Hork-Bajir Chronicles (entry 3)** is described as a companion book to *Animorphs*, focusing on the enslaved Hork-Bajir species and their world, narrated from the perspectives of enslaved characters.  
- Other entries (e.g., *Left Behind: The Kids*, *Shadowshaper*) do not align with all criteria (e.g., lack science fantasy label, first-person narrative, or focus on enslaved alien worlds).  

Options:  
- The primary candidate is *Animorphs* with its companion *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles*, as they meet the science fantasy, first-person, and enslaved alien world criteria.  
- Alternative possibilities include Victoria Hanley’s companion books (entry 2) or Square Enix companion works (entry 6), but these lack explicit ties to enslaved alien species or science fantasy framing.  

Assumptions:  
- **Companion books** in the question refer to works directly tied to the main series (e.g., *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* as a companion to *Animorphs*).  
- The term "science fantasy" in the question aligns with the genre label applied to *Animorphs* (entry 9), even though other entries (e.g., *Etiquette & Espionage*) blend genres.  
- The enslaved Hork-Bajir in *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* are explicitly tied to the broader *Animorphs* narrative, though the context does not clarify if this is a standalone companion or part of a larger series.  

Open Questions:  
- Is *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* a standalone companion book or part of a larger series tied to *Animorphs*?  
- Could other entries (e.g., Square Enix companion books) be interpreted as science fantasy with enslaved alien themes, even if not explicitly labeled as such?  
- Are there unresolved ambiguities about whether "companion books" in the question refer to supplementary works or standalone series?  

Research Handoff:  
Prioritize verifying the relationship between *Animorphs* and *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* (e.g., whether the latter is a direct companion series) and confirming that no other entry meets all criteria (science fantasy, first-person, enslaved alien worlds). Focus on entries 3 and 9, with secondary review of entries 2 and 6 for alternative possibilities.
</researcher_notes>

Synthesize the research into the strongest draft answer you can. Be decisive, but preserve caveats where evidence is weak. Make the draft match the requested deliverable.
steps.response:
Core Answer:  
The *Animorphs* series, with its companion book *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles*, meets the criteria of a science fantasy young adult series told in first person, featuring stories of enslaved alien species.  

Reasoning:  
- **Animorphs (entry 9)** is explicitly labeled as a "science fantasy" series, written for young adults, and told in first person with multiple narrators.  
- **The Hork-Bajir Chronicles (entry 3)** is described as a companion book to *Animorphs*, focusing on the enslaved Hork-Bajir species and their world, narrated from the perspectives of enslaved characters. This directly addresses the "stories of enslaved worlds and alien species" criterion.  
- No other entry in the context matches all criteria (science fantasy, first-person narrative, and enslaved alien worlds) as explicitly.  

Caveats:  
- The term "companion books" in the question may refer to standalone works rather than a set, though *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is explicitly tied to *Animorphs*.  
- The enslaved Hork-Bajir are part of the broader *Animorphs* narrative, but the context does not clarify if *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is a standalone companion or part of a larger series.  
- While *Animorphs* is labeled as "science fantasy," other entries (e.g., *Etiquette & Espionage*) blend genres, but they lack the enslaved alien world focus.  

Draft Deliverable:  
The *Animorphs* series, written by K. A. Applegate, is a science fantasy young adult series told in first person. Its companion book, *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles*, narrates the story of the enslaved Hork-Bajir species and their world, fulfilling the criteria of enslaved alien worlds and first-person narration.

[1] Andre Norton Award
The Andre Norton Award for Young Adult Science Fiction and Fantasy is an annual award presented by the Science Fiction and Fantasy Writers of America (SFWA) to the author of the best young adult or middle grade science fiction or fantasy book published in the United States in the preceding year. It is named to honor prolific science fiction and fantasy author Andre Norton (1912–2005), and it was established by then SFWA president Catherine Asaro and the SFWA Young Adult Fiction committee and announced on February 20, 2005. Any published young adult or middle grade science fiction or fantasy novel is eligible for the prize, including graphic novels. There is no limit on word count. The award is presented along with the Nebula Awards and follows the same rules for nominations and voting; as the awards are separate, works may be simultaneously nominated for both the Andre Norton award and a Nebula Award.

[2] Victoria Hanley

Her newest book (released March 2012) is the sequel of a series, called "Indigo Magic", published by Egmont USA. She's also published two non-fiction books through Cotton Wood Press; called "Seize the Story: A Handbook For Teens Who Like To Write", and "Wild Ink: A Grownups Guide To Writing Fiction For Teens".

steps.response:
Major Issues:  
- The draft assumes *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is part of a "set of companion books," but the context only explicitly identifies it as a single companion book to *Animorphs*, not a series of interconnected works. This overreaches the evidence.  

Minor Issues:  
- The draft notes *Animorphs* uses "multiple narrators" for first-person narration, but the question specifies "told in first person" without clarifying if multiple perspectives are acceptable. This could be interpreted as a minor ambiguity.  

Missing Evidence:  
- No context confirms the existence of additional companion books beyond *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* that collectively form a "set" of works about enslaved alien worlds. The draft infers a set based on a single example.  

Required Revisions:  
- Clarify that *The Hork-Bajir Chronicles* is a single companion book, not a set, unless other unmentioned works exist. Adjust the answer to reflect that the question’s "set of companion books" criterion is not fully met by the evidence.

They produced three books between 1998 and 1999 before the line was stopped in favor of the "Ultimania" (アルティマニア , Arutimania ) series, a portmanteau of ultimate and mania. This series of books is written by Studio BentStuff, which had previously written game guides for Square for "Final Fantasy VII". They were published by DigiCube until the company was dissolved in 2003. Square merged with video game publisher Enix on April 1, 2003 to form Square Enix, which resumed publication of the companion books.

[7] The Divide trilogy
The Divide trilogy is a fantasy young adult novel trilogy by Elizabeth Kay, which takes place in an alternate universe. The three books are "The Divide" (2002), "Back to The Divide" (2005), and "Jinx on The Divide" (2006). The first novel was originally published by the small press publisher Chicken House (now a division of Scholastic), with subsequent volumes published by Scholastic, which also reprinted the first novel. The books have been translated into French, German, Spanish, Finnish, Chinese, Japanese, Portuguese, Italian, Romanian and Dutch. Interior illustrations are by Ted Dewan.

[8] Science Fantasy (magazine)
Science Fantasy, which also appeared under the titles Impulse and SF Impulse, was a British fantasy and science fiction magazine, launched in 1950 by Nova Publications as a companion to Nova's "New Worlds". Walter Gillings was editor for the first two issues, and was then replaced by John Carnell, the editor of "New Worlds", as a cost-saving measure. Carnell edited both magazines until Nova went out of business in early 1964. The titles were acquired by Roberts & Vinter, who hired Kyril Bonfiglioli to edit "Science Fantasy"; Bonfiglioli changed the title to "Impulse" in early 1966, but the new title led to confusion with the distributors and sales fell, though the magazine remained profitable. The title was changed again to "SF Impulse" for the last few issues. "Science Fantasy" ceased publication the following year, when Roberts & Vinter came under financial pressure after their printer went bankrupt.

[9] Animorphs
Animorphs is a science fantasy series of young adult books written by Katherine Applegate and her husband Michael Grant, writing together under the name K. A. Applegate, and published by Scholastic. It is told in first person, with all six main characters taking turns narrating the books through their own perspectives. Horror, war, dehumanization, sanity, morality, innocence, leadership, freedom and growing up are the core themes of the series.

[10] Etiquette & Espionage
Etiquette & Espionage is a young adult steampunk novel by Gail Carriger. It is her first young adult novel, and is set in the same universe as her bestselling Parasol Protectorate adult series.
